text: Embeddings are dense numerical representations of data, typically text, in a continuous vector space. Each word, sentence, or document is mapped to a fixed-length array of floating-point numbers. These vectors capture semantic meaning so that similar concepts are located close together in the vector space. Text is converted to vectors by passing it through a neural network trained on large corpora. Models such as Word2Vec, GloVe, and sentence-transformers learn to produce vectors by predicting context words or by optimizing for semantic similarity on labeled datasets. The result is that the word "king" minus "man" plus "woman" produces a vector close to "queen." The practical value of embeddings is that mathematical operations on vectors correspond to semantic relationships in language. Cosine similarity between two vectors measures how semantically related two pieces of text are. This property underpins semantic search, recommendation systems, and retrieval-augmented generation pipelines.

TCP, the Transmission Control Protocol, is a connection-oriented protocol that guarantees reliable, ordered delivery of data between two hosts. Before data is transmitted, TCP performs a three-way handshake to establish a connection. It uses acknowledgments, retransmissions, and flow control to ensure every packet arrives correctly and in order. TCP is used by applications where data integrity is critical, such as web browsing, email, and file transfer. UDP, the User Datagram Protocol, is a connectionless protocol that sends packets without establishing a connection or guaranteeing delivery. There is no handshake, no acknowledgment, and no retransmission. UDP has lower latency and overhead than TCP, making it suitable for applications where speed matters more than reliability, such as video streaming, online gaming, and DNS lookups. Data transmission over a network is divided into layers described by models such as the OSI model. The transport layer, where TCP and UDP operate, is responsible for end-to-end communication between processes. Below it, the network layer handles routing packets across networks using IP addresses. The physical and data link layers handle the actual transmission of bits over physical media.
routing packets across networks using IP addresses. The physical and data link layers handle the actual transmission of bits over physical media.

Binary search finds a target value in a sorted array by repeatedly halving the search interval. It compares the target to the middle element and eliminates the half where the target cannot exist. Binary search runs in O(log n) time and O(1) space, making it far more efficient than linear search for large sorted datasets. Merge sort is a divide-and-conquer sorting algorithm that splits an array into halves, recursively sorts each half, and merges the sorted halves back together. It runs in O(n log n) time in all cases and uses O(n) auxiliary space. Merge sort is stable, meaning equal elements preserve their original relative order. Quicksort selects a pivot element and partitions the array into elements less than and greater than the pivot, then recursively sorts each partition. Its average-case time complexity is O(n log n), but its worst case is O(n²) when the pivot is consistently the smallest or largest element. In-place variants use O(log n) stack space. Dijkstra's algorithm finds the shortest path from a source node to all other nodes in a weighted graph with non-negative edge weights. It uses a priority queue to greedily expand the nearest unvisited node. The time complexity is O((V +

Dijkstra's algorithm finds the shortest path from a source node to all other nodes in a weighted graph with non-negative edge weights. It uses a priority queue to greedily expand the nearest unvisited node. The time complexity is O((V + E) log V) with a binary heap, where V is the number of vertices and E is the number of edges. Breadth-first search (BFS) explores a graph level by level, visiting all neighbors of a node before moving to the next depth. It uses a queue and runs in O(V + E) time. BFS finds the shortest path in an unweighted graph and is used for cycle detection and connected component discovery. Dynamic programming solves problems by breaking them into overlapping subproblems and storing their results to avoid redundant computation. It applies when a problem has optimal substructure and overlapping subproblems. Classic examples include the Fibonacci sequence, the 0/1 knapsack problem, and longest common subsequence. A greedy algorithm makes the locally optimal choice at each step with the hope of reaching a global optimum. Greedy algorithms do not always produce optimal solutions but work correctly for problems like Huffman coding, activity selection, and Kruskal's minimum spanning tree algorithm.

A process is an instance of a running program. It has its own memory space, file handles, and system resources. A thread is a lightweight unit of execution within a process that shares the process's memory and resources. Threads within the same process communicate via shared memory, while processes communicate through inter-process communication mechanisms like pipes and sockets. A deadlock occurs when two or more processes are each waiting for a resource held by the other, causing all of them to block indefinitely. The four necessary conditions for deadlock are mutual exclusion, hold and wait, no preemption, and circular wait. Deadlock can be prevented by breaking any one of these conditions. Virtual memory allows a process to use more memory than is physically available by mapping virtual addresses to physical RAM or disk storage. The operating system uses a page table to translate virtual addresses to physical addresses. Pages not in RAM are stored on disk and loaded on demand, a mechanism called demand paging. A context switch occurs when the CPU switches from executing one process or thread to another. The OS saves the current process's register state and loads the saved state of the next process. Context switches

a mechanism called demand paging. A context switch occurs when the CPU switches from executing one process or thread to another. The OS saves the current process's register state and loads the saved state of the next process. Context switches are necessary for multitasking but introduce overhead since they require saving and restoring CPU state. Scheduling algorithms determine the order in which processes are given CPU time. First-come first-served (FCFS) processes jobs in arrival order. Round-robin assigns each process a fixed time quantum in rotation. Shortest job first (SJF) minimizes average waiting time but requires knowing execution time in advance. A mutex (mutual exclusion lock) is a synchronization primitive that ensures only one thread accesses a critical section at a time. A semaphore is a more general synchronization mechanism that maintains a counter and supports signaling between threads. Mutexes are typically used for exclusive access, while semaphores are used for signaling or limiting concurrent access to a resource pool. Paging divides physical memory into fixed-size frames and virtual memory into pages of the same size. When a process accesses a page not in RAM, a page fault occurs and the OS loads the page from disk. Page replacement algorithms such

divides physical memory into fixed-size frames and virtual memory into pages of the same size. When a process accesses a page not in RAM, a page fault occurs and the OS loads the page from disk. Page replacement algorithms such as LRU (least recently used) and FIFO determine which page to evict when memory is full.

Transformers are a deep learning architecture introduced in the 2017 paper "Attention Is All You Need" by Vaswani et al. Unlike recurrent neural networks, transformers process entire sequences simultaneously rather than step by step, making them significantly faster to train on modern hardware. The core mechanism of a transformer is self-attention, which allows every token in a sequence to attend to every other token. Self-attention computes a weighted sum of values based on the similarity between queries and keys, enabling the model to capture long-range dependencies regardless of the distance between tokens in the sequence. Transformers have become the dominant architecture in natural language processing. Large language models such as GPT, BERT, and LLaMA are all built on the transformer architecture. They are used for tasks including text generation, translation, summarization, question answering, and code completion.

Vector databases are storage systems designed to index and search high-dimensional vectors efficiently. Unlike traditional relational databases that search by exact match, vector databases retrieve the nearest neighbors to a query vector using approximate nearest neighbor algorithms. Similarity search works by computing a distance metric, most commonly cosine similarity or Euclidean distance, between the query vector and all stored vectors. Because computing this over millions of vectors naively is slow, vector databases use indexing structures such as HNSW or inverted file indexes to reduce the search space. FAISS, developed by Meta AI Research, is one of the most widely used vector similarity search libraries. It supports both exact and approximate search and runs efficiently on CPUs and GPUs. Other popular vector databases include Pinecone, Weaviate, and Chroma, which add persistence, filtering, and cloud hosting on top of similar search functionality.

The Earth's average surface temperature has increased by approximately 1.1 degrees Celsius since the late 19th century. This warming is primarily driven by human activities, especially the burning of fossil fuels like coal, oil, and natural gas. The Intergovernmental Panel on Climate Change (IPCC) has concluded that human influence is the dominant cause of observed warming since the mid-20th century. Rising temperatures are linked to more frequent extreme weather events, melting ice caps, and rising sea levels.

A hash table is a data structure that maps keys to values using a hash function. The hash function converts a key into an integer index, which determines where the value is stored in an underlying array. Average-case lookup, insertion, and deletion all run in O(1) time, making hash tables one of the most efficient structures for key-value storage. Collisions, where two keys hash to the same index, are handled through chaining or open addressing. Arrays store elements in contiguous memory locations and provide O(1) access by index. They are the foundation of many higher-level data structures. Dynamic arrays, such as Python lists, resize automatically when capacity is exceeded, amortizing the cost of resizing over many insertions. Binary search trees store elements in sorted order such that each node's left subtree contains smaller values and the right subtree contains larger values. Balanced variants such as AVL trees and red-black trees guarantee O(log n) time for search, insertion, and deletion by maintaining bounded tree height.
